title: Recursion
description: Recursion in C — base cases, memoization with arrays, backtracking patterns, and converting to iterative solutions for interview problems.

sections.title: Base case and recursive calls
sections.explanation: Every recursive function needs a **base case** that stops recursion and a **recursive case** that moves toward it. In C, watch for stack overflow with deep recursion — the default stack is limited. Always ensure your recursive calls make progress toward the base case.

exercise.prompt: Write a recursive function `sum_to_n` that returns the sum 1 + 2 + ... + n. Base case: n <= 0 returns 0. Print sum_to_n(10).

validation.message: Use base case n <= 0 returning 0, recurse with n + sum_to_n(n-1)

exercise.hint: Base case: n <= 0 returns 0. Recursive: return n + sum_to_n(n-1). sum_to_n(10) = 55.

sections.title: Memoization with arrays
sections.explanation: Naive recursion often recomputes the same subproblems. **Memoization** caches results in an array: check `if (memo[n] != -1) return memo[n]` before computing. Initialize with `memset(memo, -1, sizeof(memo))`. This turns exponential Fibonacci into O(n).

exercise.prompt: Compute fibonacci(30) using memoization with a global array. Print the result. Without memoization this would be very slow; with it, it's instant.

validation.message: Check memo before computing, store result in memo, initialize with -1

exercise.hint: Initialize memo with -1 using memset. Check memo[n] != -1 before recursing. fib(30) = 832040.

sections.title: Backtracking pattern
sections.explanation: Backtracking explores all possibilities by making a choice, recursing, then **undoing the choice** (backtracking). Use a path/state array, mark choices, recurse, then unmark. This pattern solves permutations, combinations, and constraint satisfaction problems.

exercise.prompt: Generate all permutations of {1, 2, 3} using backtracking. Print each permutation on a line, elements space-separated.

validation.message: All 6 permutations of {1,2,3} should be printed
Mark used before recursion, unmark after (backtrack), store choice in path

exercise.hint: Mark used[i] = 1 before recursing, set used[i] = 0 after to backtrack. There are 3! = 6 permutations.

sections.title: Converting recursion to iterative
sections.explanation: Some interviewers ask for iterative solutions. Convert recursion by using an explicit stack (array) that simulates the call stack. Push initial state, loop while stack not empty, pop and process. Fibonacci converts to a simple loop with two variables.

exercise.prompt: Convert the recursive factorial to an iterative version using a for loop. Compute and print factorial(10).

validation.message: Use an iterative loop multiplying result by i from 2 to n

exercise.hint: Start result = 1. Multiply by each i from 2 to n. 10! = 3628800.
